narrative: Late Saturday, the city winding down. Marcus is parked outside the refinery, engine running, writing the incident report on his phone with his thumbs. Not the shift supervisor’s version. His version. The one with the dates, the readings, the specific language from the safety manual that says what should have happened. He knows what filing it means. He files it anyway, attaching it to the corporate safety inbox and his personal email at 11:14 PM, then gets out of the car and walks into his shift like a man who just lit a match near something flammable. Out at the ranch, Cole checks the gate before bed and the dog is still there — but closer now, maybe ten feet from the porch, curled in a spot where the snow melted during the day. Cole sets out another bowl of food and a folded horse blanket and goes inside without looking back. The rest of the city sleeps. Jolene’s light is off. Nora’s alarm is set for five. Eli is lying in bed staring at the ceiling, replaying the sound of the applause.
events: Marcus sits in his car outside the refinery for twenty minutes before his shift, writing an incident report on his phone
Cole’s stray dog is still at the ranch gate — it ate the food, moved ten feet closer to the house
